metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Genocide Studies 12 examines the origins, characteristics, and global manifestations of genocide, alongside responses and prevention strategies. Students explore how genocides occur, what conditions make them possible, and how international law and civil society have tried — and often failed — to prevent them. The course builds critical thinking skills, emotional literacy, and a commitment to human dignity.
metadata.general_description: A study of genocide as a historical and contemporary phenomenon, examining its stages, global examples, international legal responses, and prevention strategies — grounded in a commitment to human dignity and never again.

big_ideas.statement: The intentional destruction of peoples and their cultures is not inevitable, and such attempts can be disrupted and resisted.
big_ideas.elaboration: Genocide requires political will, organization, and public complicity — all of which can be identified and interrupted at multiple stages.

big_ideas.statement: The use of the term 'genocide' to describe atrocities has political, legal, social, and cultural ramifications.
big_ideas.elaboration: Calling something 'genocide' carries legal weight, political consequences, and moral significance — and is therefore fiercely contested.

big_ideas.statement: Despite international commitments to prohibit genocide, violence targeted against groups of people has continued to challenge global peace and prosperity.
big_ideas.elaboration: The UN Genocide Convention (1948) has not prevented subsequent genocides, revealing the gap between international norms and political will.

big_ideas.statement: While genocides are caused by and carried out for different reasons, all genocides share similarities in progression and scope.
big_ideas.elaboration: From classification and symbolization to denial, each genocide follows recognizable patterns that enable early warning and intervention.

categories.name: Inquiry Processes and Skills
categories.raw: Drawing conclusions, assessing positions, identifying problems, organizing data, interpreting maps/graphs, citing sources accurately.

competencies.raw: Use inquiry processes to investigate genocide: draw conclusions, assess positions, interpret data, cite sources.
i_can_statements: I can develop a research question about genocide and conduct structured inquiry.
I can interpret maps, charts, and survivor testimonies as sources.
I can cite historical and legal sources accurately.
I can draw reasoned conclusions from evidence about why genocides occur.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Ranking importance using criteria; comparing group perspectives on historical relevance.

competencies.raw: Assess the significance of genocides and compare how different groups view their historical importance.
i_can_statements: I can apply criteria for significance to evaluate a specific genocide's historical impact.
I can explain why some genocides receive more international attention and recognition than others.
I can compare how survivors, perpetrators, and bystanders view the significance of the same event.

categories.name: Evidence Credibility
categories.raw: Evaluating source reliability, evidence adequacy, account bias, comparing multiple accounts.

competencies.raw: Evaluate the credibility and reliability of sources on genocide, including survivor testimony and denial claims.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate survivor testimony as historical evidence, considering trauma, memory, and perspective.
I can identify denial claims about genocide and explain how historians assess their credibility.
I can compare multiple accounts of the same event and explain why they differ.
I can analyze photographic and forensic evidence as forms of historical proof.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Analyzing differential impacts on groups; examining gradual versus sudden change.

competencies.raw: Analyze how different groups were affected by genocide across different time periods.
i_can_statements: I can trace how a genocide unfolded over time, from early warning signs to implementation.
I can compare how different groups (perpetrators, victims, bystanders, international community) experienced the same genocide.
I can analyze whether international responses to genocide have changed over the 20th and 21st centuries.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Assessing individual/group influence; distinguishing intended from unintended outcomes.

competencies.raw: Assess the causes and consequences of genocide, distinguishing intended from unintended outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can identify the political, economic, social, and psychological conditions that enabled a specific genocide.
I can evaluate the role of leadership, propaganda, and ordinary citizens in carrying out genocide.
I can trace the long-term consequences of genocide on surviving communities and the world.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explaining differing viewpoints through norms, values, worldviews, and beliefs.

competencies.raw: Explain differing perspectives on genocide through norms, values, worldviews, and beliefs.
i_can_statements: I can explain the perspective of perpetrators, bystanders, rescuers, and victims in a specific genocide.
I can analyze how nationalism and dehumanizing ideology shaped perpetrators' worldviews.
I can discuss why bystanders often fail to intervene, and what might change this.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Making reasoned assessments of actions; considering historical context in moral evaluation.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about genocide, considering historical context.
i_can_statements: I can make an ethical judgment about the responsibility of bystanders during genocide.
I can evaluate whether international responses to a specific genocide were ethically adequate.
I can argue for the moral imperative of genocide recognition and prevention.

areas.name: Origins and Development of the Term 'Genocide'
areas.raw: Understanding the term 'genocide' and its emergence.
topics: Raphael Lemkin and the coining of the word 'genocide' (1944)
The UN Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide (1948)
The legal definition of genocide vs. crimes against humanity and ethnic cleansing
Political debates around what qualifies as genocide
Cultural genocide: definition and examples including residential schools

areas.name: Conditions That Enable Genocide
areas.raw: Economic, political, social, and cultural factors; perpetrators, leaders, vulnerable minorities.
topics: Economic instability and scapegoating minority groups
Political systems that concentrate power and remove checks and balances
The role of nationalism and ethnic or religious 'othering'
The psychology of perpetrators: ordinary people and extraordinary evil
The role of leaders in authorizing and directing genocide
Vulnerable minorities: who is targeted and why

areas.name: Characteristics and Stages of Genocide
topics: Gregory Stanton's 10 Stages of Genocide: classification, symbolization, discrimination, dehumanization, organization, polarization, preparation, persecution, extermination, denial
The role of propaganda in each stage
Early warning signs and opportunities for intervention
The bureaucratic organization of mass murder
The role of technology in enabling genocide

areas.name: Global Examples of Genocide
areas.raw: Indigenous peoples, Beothuk extinction, Armenian genocide, Holocaust, Cambodia, Rwanda, Sudan, Guatemala, Yugoslavia, and others.
topics: Cultural genocide against Indigenous peoples: Canada, Australia, Americas
The extinction of the Beothuk people of Newfoundland
The Armenian Genocide (1915–1923): Ottoman Empire
The Holocaust (1933–1945): Nazi Germany and occupied Europe
The Cambodian Genocide (1975–1979): Khmer Rouge
The Rwandan Genocide (1994): Hutu and Tutsi
Darfur, Sudan (2003–present)
The Guatemalan Civil War and genocide of Maya peoples
The Bosnian Genocide and the siege of Srebrenica (1992–1995)
The Uyghur situation in China: ongoing debates and evidence

areas.name: Strategies Used in Genocide
areas.raw: Rape, propaganda, dehumanization, organized violence, starvation, denial of rights.
topics: Sexual violence as a weapon of genocide
Propaganda and dehumanization: how language enables killing
Starvation as a weapon: the Holodomor, the Bengal Famine
Mass graves, execution sites, and organized killing
Denial of rights as a precursor to genocide
Forced displacement and ethnic cleansing

areas.name: The Role of Technology
areas.raw: How technology promotes and facilitates genocidal acts.
topics: The role of the radio in the Rwandan Genocide
Nazi use of technology: bureaucracy, transport, industrialized killing
Social media and hate speech in modern genocides
Satellite imagery and documentation of atrocities
Digital tools for human rights monitoring

areas.name: Recognition and Responses
topics: The Nuremberg Trials: establishing precedents for international law
The International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR)
The International Criminal Court (ICC) and its limitations
Reparations and survivor compensation
Memorials and museums: Yad Vashem, the USHMM, Kigali Genocide Memorial
Reconciliation processes: Rwanda's Gacaca courts, TRC in Canada

areas.name: Denial Movements
areas.raw: Methods and credibility assessment of denial claims.
topics: Holocaust denial: history, methods, and legal status
Armenian Genocide denial: political uses by Turkey
How historians evaluate and refute denial claims
The role of states in institutionalizing denial
Digital disinformation and genocide denial

areas.name: Prevention
areas.raw: International legal frameworks and enforcement mechanisms.
topics: The Responsibility to Protect (R2P) doctrine
Early warning systems: Genocide Watch, UN Special Adviser
The role of civil society in genocide prevention
Education as a prevention tool: Holocaust and genocide education
Bystander intervention: individual and collective responsibility
